coredata.dc:title: Corrosion inhibition of eco-friendly nitrogen-doped carbon dots for carbon steel in acidic media: Performance and mechanism investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Mengyue
He, Zhongyi
Guo, Lei
Zhang, Renhui
Anadebe, Valentine Chikaodili
Obot, Ime Bassey
Zheng, Xingwen
coredata.dc:description: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were obtained by hydrothermal method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. The corrosion inhibition performance and mechanism of prepared the NCDs on the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution were evaluated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization curve, and surface analysis. The results that the NCDs exhibited 93% of inhibition efficiency at 100 mg/L. Electrochemical measurements demonstrated that the NCDs adsorbed on the metal surface and effectively inhibited the electron transfer process. The potentiodynamic polarization suggested that NCDs were excellent mix-type corrosion inhibition. Furthermore, NCDs successfully prevented the dissolution of anode metal and the evolution of cathode hydrogen ions. The adsorption of the NCDs followed Langmuir adsorption isotherm indicated physical and chemical adsorption. At the same time, the adsorption mechanism was analyzed, guiding the use of carbon dots as environment-friendly corrosion inhibitors in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Carbon steel
Corrosion inhibition
Electrochemistry
Adsorption mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21706195, 22062022), the Foundation of the Department of Education of Guizhou province (QJHKY[2020]067, KY[2018]030), and the Opening Project of Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan province (2020CL06).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Main properties and applications of CDs.
Schematic diagram of NCDs preparation process.
UV–Vis, FTIR, and XPS spectra of NCDs. (a) UV–vis, (b) FTIR, (c) XPS-NCDs, (d) XPS-C1s, (e) XPS-N1s, and (f) XPS-O1s.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) TEM image of the as-prepared NCDs.
Nyquist and Bode plots of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence of presence of different concentrations of NCDs at 298 K. (Inset: equivalent circuit diagram).
Potentiodynamic polarization plots of Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution in the absence of presence of different concentrations of NCDs at 298 K.
XPS spectra of Q235 steel surface in blank and NCDs-contained solutions. (a, b) full XPS spectra, (c) C 1 s, and (d) N 1 s spectra.
Langmuir adsorption isotherms of Q235 steel surface.
SEM-EDS, contact angle, and AFM results for Q235 steel surface under different conditions.
Mechanism of the NCDs inhibitor in 1 M HCl solution on the surface of Q235 Steel.
Impedance parameters of Q235 steel with different concentration of corrosion inhibitor in 1 M HCl solution.
Potentiodynamic polarization parameters of Q235 steel with different concentration of corrosion inhibitor in 1 M HCl solution.

head.ce:title: Corrosion inhibition of eco-friendly nitrogen-doped carbon dots for carbon steel in acidic media: Performance and mechanism investigation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering, Zigong 643000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were obtained by hydrothermal method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. The corrosion inhibition performance and mechanism of prepared the NCDs on the Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution were evaluated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), potentiodynamic polarization curve, and surface analysis. The results that the NCDs exhibited 93% of inhibition efficiency at 100 mg/L. Electrochemical measurements demonstrated that the NCDs adsorbed on the metal surface and effectively inhibited the electron transfer process. The potentiodynamic polarization suggested that NCDs were excellent mix-type corrosion inhibition. Furthermore, NCDs successfully prevented the dissolution of anode metal and the evolution of cathode hydrogen ions. The adsorption of the NCDs followed Langmuir adsorption isotherm indicated physical and chemical adsorption. At the same time, the adsorption mechanism was analyzed, guiding the use of carbon dots as environment-friendly corrosion inhibitors in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon steel is widely used in construction, piping, transportation, and marine owing to its low cost, high flexibility, and good weldability [1–4]. However, carbon steel is prone to corrosion under acidic (1 M HCl or 0.5 M H2SO4 solution), neutral (3.5 wt% NaCl solution), and alkaline (NaOH solution) conditions because of low carbon content [5–8]. In industry, carbon steels are frequently protected by coating, electroplating, hot-plating, and corrosion inhibitors. Among them, oil wells, natural gas, and drainage pipelines create dirt, deposition, and rust in the process of working [9,10]. As a result, the pipeline is frequently cleaned with a high concentration of hydrochloric acid. On the other hand, hydrochloric acid is readily reacted with metal, resulting in hydrogen embrittlement, which affects the service life of carbon steel [11]. Hence, different types of corrosion inhibitors are developed to mitigate the metal corrosion in the industry. In the past, inorganic compounds such as chromate [12], nitrate, and molybdate [13] were used as oxidizing corrosion inhibitors. However, Inorganic corrosion inhibitors contained high-pollution heavy metal components that could endanger human health and the environment. In response to the world environmental problems, researchers have devoted increasing attention to the low toxicity, less-polluting, and friendly environmental corrosion inhibitor in recent years.

Carbon dots are a type of zero-dimensional substance with a diameter of fewer than 10 nm. Fig. 1 depicts the key features and applications of carbon dots. The synthesis methods of carbon dots are generally divided into two types that top-down and bottom-up [14]. The bottom-up method is divided into hydrothermal [15], electrolytic [16], and microwave methods [17], etc. The method is small molecules are sequentially decomposed, assembled, and aggregated to form spherical or elliptical carbon particles. The carbon dots are water-soluble nanomaterials, biocompatible, environmentally friendly, fluorescence stable, and inexpensive [18–20]. Based on the many advantages of carbon dots, it has become a hot topic for researchers to explore. Applications of carbon dots in bioimaging [21,22], battery energy storage [23], catalysts [24], and environmental monitoring [25] have been widely reported. However, the carbon dots as corrosion inhibitors have been relatively rare. In recent years, Cui et al. prepared nitrogen-doped quantum dots using o-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine as precursors, and explored their corrosion inhibition performance [26,27]. The results illustrate that the two kinds of N-doped carbon quantum dots slowed down the corrosion of metal. The corrosion inhibition efficiency reached up to 97% by increasing the immersion time. Langmuir adsorption isotherm displayed that the corrosion inhibitor protected the metal surface by physical and chemical adsorption. Cen et al. reported that the preparation of N, S-CDs using 4-aminosalicylic acid and thiourea as precursors, and studied corrosion inhibition effect of inhibitor on carbon steel in CO2-saturated 3.5% NaCl solution at 50 ℃ [28]. The results indicated that the corrosion inhibition effect was superior under the lower concentration of corrosion inhibitor. The N, S-CDs were chemically adsorbed on the metal surface, preventing the corrosion from the electrolyte. N-doped quantum dots synthesized from methacrylic acid and n-butylamine were also prepared successively by Ye et al. [29]. The corrosion inhibition was further studied by electrochemical measurement and surface analysis. The results elucidated that the corrosion of carbon steel surface was inhibited by the formation of barrier membrane that the physical and chemical interaction between the N-doped quantum dots inhibitor and the metal surface. Luo and collaborators discussed the N-CDs generated at different synthesis time [30]. They found that N-CDs synthesized for 1 h had the best corrosion inhibition effect, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency reached 90%. Saraswat and colleagues synthesized N, S-doped quantum dots using isoniazid and thiourea as precursors to provide nitrogen and sulfur [31]. Corrosion inhibition efficiency on mild steel in 15% HCl solution was 98.64% at 225 mg/L N, S-CDs. The current research on carbon dots as corrosion inhibitors has been reported continuously. However, the precursors of synthesizing carbon dots require to be further selected considering the cost. Carbon dots with high corrosion inhibition performance are also urgently needed to be developed. Therefore, we chose cheap organic reagents as precursors and a simple hydrothermal method to synthesize N-doped carbon dots with high yield, low toxicity, and high corrosion inhibition in this paper.

This prepared N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) using cheap and easy-to-get citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. The structure and properties of NCDs were characterized by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The corrosion inhibition behavior of Q235 steel by NCDs in 1 M HCl solution was measured by electrochemical measurement, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), XPS, atomic force microscopy (AFM), and contact angle measuring instrument. The adsorption mechanism of NCDs was analyzed by Langmuir adsorption isotherm.

A hydrated citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) pure analytical grades were purchased from Aladdin Reagents Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (36%∼38%) were provided by Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The size of Q235 steel was Φ16 × 5 mm purchased by local suppliers. The Q235 steel was used as a working electrode by ultrasonic cleaning with anhydrous ethanol and hydro-polishing with 120, 240, 400, 600, 800, 1000, and 1200 mesh sandpaper.

The preparation process of NCDs was shown in Fig. 2 . The detailed steps were as follows: 6.301 g (30 mmol) of hydrated citric acid and 2 mL (30 mmol) of ethylenediamine were completely dissolved in 60 mL of deionized water. The aqueous solution was added to a 100 mL Teflon reactor, and then heated at 200 ℃ for 5 h. After the reaction, it was put to room temperature. The large particles after the reaction were removed successively through 0.45 μm, and 0.22 μm microporous filtration membranes. Then, the unreacted small molecules were removed by dialysis bag with an average molecular weight of 1000 for 24 h to achieve further purification. Finally, most of the moisture was removed by rotary evaporation at 60 ℃. The brown powder was obtained by freeze-drying for 48 h.

The Irtracer-100 Fourier Transform Spectrometer and the Tu-1901 Dual-beam UV–vis Spectrophotometer were used to examine the structure of NCDs. Thermo Fisher Scientific K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were utilized to analyze the bonding patterns of NCDs. XRD-6100 diffractometer and JEM-2010 transmission electron microscopy were used to determine the crystal state and morphology of NCDs.

Electrochemical measurements of the Q235 carbon steel at different concentrations of NCDs in 1 M HCl solutions were recorded by CHI-660E electrochemical workstation. The traditional three-electrode system was used for the test. The Q235 carbon steel with the contact surface of 1 cm2 was used as the working electrode, the counter electrode was stable platinum, and the saturated calomel was utilized as the reference electrode. Before the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Tafel curves measurement, the working electrode should be measured at an open circuit potential of 1200 s to achieve a stable state. After that, the sample was tested by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy under the premise of frequency of 105 ∼ 10−2 Hz, and amplitude of 5 mV. The potentiodynamic polarization curves were measured at a scanning rate of 0.5 mV and a voltage range of ± 250 mV relative to the open circuit potential. The electrochemical impedance data were fitted by ZsimpWin 3.6 software to simulate the equivalent circuit. All tests in the experiment were carried out three times in parallel to reduce the experimental operation error and increase the reliability of the data.

The Q235 steel was polished by sandpaper with 120 ∼ 5000 grits, immersed in 1 M HCl solution of 100 mg/L NCDs for 6 h, followed by ultrasonic cleaning with deionized water, anhydrous ethanol and acetone, and cold air drying. The surface morphology was photographed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL-JSM-7800F). XPS characterized the bonding way of NCDs adsorption films on the metal surface. The roughness and wetness of the metal surface were evaluated by atomic force microscopy (MFP-3D-BIO) and contact Angle measurement (SCI2000B).

The UV–vis, FTIR, and XPS of NCDs display in Fig. 3 . The UV–vis spectra of NCDs shows in Fig. 3a. The UV–Vis absorption of NCDs has characteristic peaks at 241 nm and 338 nm. It can be interpreted as 241 nm is the π-π* electron transition of the conjugated structure in NCDs, while 338 nm corresponds to the n-π* electron transition of the C = N bond [32]. As shown in Fig. 3b, the FTIR spectra of hydrated citric acid (CA) at the peaks 3337 cm−1,1699 cm−1, 1411 cm−1, and 1210 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of − OH, C = O, the shear vibration of CH2, and the stretching vibration of C − OH, respectively [33,34]. However, for the NCDs, its peaks at 1667 cm−1, 1543 cm−1, 1362 cm−1, and 756 cm−1 are assigned to the − CONH − stretching vibration of the amide I band and amide Ⅱ band, the shear vibration of − CH and the bending vibration out of the − CH plane of the benzene ring [35,36]. By comparing the FTIR spectra of NCDs with the precursor CA, it is seen that NCDs are proved to contain obvious amide and benzene ring functional groups. Combined with UV–vis absorption spectrum indicate that NCDs have been successfully prepared. Fig. 3c shows the total spectrum of XPS element of NCDs composed of three elements C, N and O. The exemplary spectrum of each element is obtained by Gaussian fitting in Fig. 3d-3f. Three peaks at 284.28 eV, 286.26 eV, and 289.63 eV are obtained by C 1 s spectrum fitting corresponding to C = C/C − C, C − N/C − O and O − C = O bonds [37], respectively. The N 1 s spectrum fitting can be allocated to 400.28 eV and 402.23 eV to match pyridinic, and the pyrrolic nitrogen [26]. The O 1 s spectrum peaks at 521.88 eV and 532.93 eV are assigned C − OH/C − O − C and C = O, respectively [21,38]. The N 1 s spectra of XPS demonstrate the formation of heterocyclic structures containing nitrogen. It corresponds with the results of UV–vis and FTIR spectra. Based on the above results, the structure of NCDs is rich functional groups containing nitrogen and oxygen atoms that increase their water solubility.

The XRD pattern of NCDs is shown in Fig. 4 a. According to the PDF card, the diffraction peak of NCDs is 2θ = 24.076°, and the lattice spacing is 0.369 nm, which corresponds to the crystal plane of nitrogen oxide graphene (003) [39]. Fig. 4b manifests the TEM image of NCDs. The NCDs present an inhomogeneous spherical and elliptical feature with a size of 6 ∼ 8 nm. It can suggest that the structure of NCDs is the stable aromatic ring and the electron-rich functional group containing nitrogen and oxygen, which is similar to the structure of nitrogen–oxygen doped graphene. Due to the doping of nitrogen and oxygen atoms, NCDs have good water solubility and dispersion that are also the key to its selection as corrosion inhibitors.

To study the corrosion inhibition effect and mechanism of corrosion inhibitor NCDs on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution, Nyquist and Bode plots in Fig. 5 were obtained by electrochemical test. As shown in the Nyquist plots, the addition of corrosion inhibitors increased the diameter of the impedance arc. When only the precursor CA is added, the impedance arc diameter is slightly increased. However, with the increase of the concentration of NCDs, the diameter of the impedance arc and the height of the capacitance arc greatly increase. It indirectly indicates that when 1 M HCl solution is added to the corrosion inhibitor, a sparse and weak barrier film forms between the solution and the metal [40]. As the concentration of NCDs increases, the protective film becomes denser, increasing in capacitive reactance value. Fig. 5b presents the Bode diagram, which contains two curves of impedance modulus and phase angle within the frequency range of 105 ∼ 10−2. From high frequency to low frequency, the impedance modulus presents an upward trend that tends to be stable after the mid-frequency region. It indicates that the impedance modulus shown in the mid-frequency region represents the whole reaction process. At this point, the higher the impedance modulus value is, the more significant the inhibition of corrosion behavior will be. It is seen from the figure that the impedance value increases correspondingly when the NCDs concentration gradient increases the intermediate frequency. It suggests that the process of charge transfer is inhibited when the corrosion inhibitor adsorbs on the metal surface [41]. There is only one peak in the phase angle diagram regardless of the addition of corrosion inhibitors. Therefore, there is only one time constant in the corrosion behavior process [42,43]. The addition of the corrosion inhibitors does not change the corrosion reaction process. The peak value increases with the increasing concentration of corrosion inhibitors in medium frequency. It is shown that the introduction of the NCDs increases the capacitance of the electrode interface, which implies that it can increase the adsorption capacity of the metal surface and forms a protective film.

According to the phase angle, the corrosion process of carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution contains only one time constant, the data of EIS is obtained by fitting the equivalent circuit, as shown in Fig. 5a. Table 1 displays relevant data of EIS after fitting. In the equivalent circuit, R s represents the solution resistance. R p is the polarization resistance. CPE is the phase angle element, which symbolizes the non-ideal capacitance, namely the capacitance value of the double electric layer at the electrode interface. The value of CPE can be calculated by equation (1) [44,45] :
(1)
Z CPE
=
Y 0
- 1
( i ω )
- n

Among them, Y 0, ω, i, and n represent the magnitude of CPE, angular frequency, imaginary unit and deviation from ideal parameter, respectively. When n is different values of − 1, 0, 0.5 and 1, CPE symbolizes different components, seen as the inductor, resistor, Warburg impedance and capacitor. χ 2 represents the goodness of fit. The C dl can be obtained according to equation (2) [46–48].
(2)
C dl
=
(
Y 0
R
P
1 - n
)
1 n

where, R p is the polarization resistance in the Nyquist diagram, and n represents the roughness of the substrate surface. As can be seen from the variation trend of n value in Table1, the n value of the blank solution is higher than that of the corrosion inhibitor. It is attributed to the uneven adsorption of corrosion inhibitors on the metal surface, which reduces the surface smoothness and increases the roughness. The data in Table 1 shows that the solution resistance R s does not change significantly before and after the addition of corrosion inhibitors, while the R p value increased remarkably and the C dl decrease clearly with the increase of NCDs concentration. It shows that a protective film is formed on the metal surface due to the adsorption of corrosion inhibitors. In addition, the Helmholtz Model equation also represents the calculation of C dl values [44,49].
(3)
C dl
=
ε ×
ε 0
d
S

wherein, ε and ε 0 represent the dielectric constant and the vacuum permittivity, respectively. S represents the effective area of the electrode; d stands for the thickness of the electric double-layer and its value is variable. The equation shows that the d value is inversely proportional to the C dl value. As the thickness of the double electric layer increases, the C dl will decrease relatively. The decreasing trend of C dl in Table 1 directly explains that the adsorption film on the metal surface thick with the increase of the concentration of corrosion inhibitor, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency η will be higher. The inhibition efficiency can be determined by Equation (4) [23].
(4)
η EIS
% =
R p
-
R
p
0
R
p
0
× 100

Thereinto, R p and R0 p are the polarization resistances without and with the inhibitor in 1 M HCl solution, respectively. When the concentration of CA is 100 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency is 48.1%, while the concentration of NCDs is 100 mg/L, the inhibition efficiency increases to 93.7%. The results show that NCDs have an excellent corrosion inhibition effect on Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution.

The potentiodynamic polarization curve of Q235 steel without and without corrosion inhibitor NCDs in 1 M HCl solution is shown in Fig. 6 . Meanwhile, the relevant parameters of the potentiodynamic polarization curve are listed in Table 2 . E corr and i corr are corrosion potential and current density, respectively. β a and β b are the relative slopes of the anodic polarization curve and the cathodic polarization curve tangent to the intersection point. θ represents the metal surface coverage, and corrosion inhibition efficiency is represented by η. The data can be obtained from Equations (5) and (6) [50].
(5)
θ =
i corr,0
-
i corr
i corr,0
(6)
η Tafel
\%
=
i corr,0
-
i corr
i corr,0
× 100
wherein, i corr,0 and i corr are the current densities absence and presence inhibitor in 1 M HCl.

It can be clearly observed in Fig. 6 that with the increase of NCDs concentration, the corrosion potential shifts to the positive direction, and the corrosion current density gradually decreases. These results indicate that the addition of corrosion inhibitor NCDs inhibits the anodic reaction at the electrode interface, thus impeding the corrosion of carbon steel. According to the data in Table 2, the corrosion potential of the NCDs inhibitor decreased by 17 mV compared with that of the blank solution, which was less than 85 mV. It is proved that NCDs belong to mixed corrosion inhibitors [51]. However, the data of anodic slope and cathodic slope show that the anode slope of corrosion inhibitor significantly changed more than that of the blank solution, while the cathode slope fluctuated less. Therefore, it is confirmed that NCDs act as a mixed corrosion inhibitor that mainly inhibits anode reaction. The surface coverage and corrosion inhibition efficiency increase when the content of the corrosion inhibitor increases. When the corrosion inhibitor is 100 mg/L, the corrosion current density decreases, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency can reach 92.1%. This is because the corrosion inhibitor adsorbs on the surface steel forming a positive film that slows down the post-oxidation dissolution reaction of the steel in the anode.

Fig. 7 illustrates the bonding characteristics of adsorption films on the surface of Q235 steel immersed in the blank solution and 100 mg/L of NCDs. The comparison between Fig. 7a and 7b shows that the N element appears on the surface of the carbon steel immersed with corrosion inhibitor, indicating that the corrosion inhibitor is successfully adsorbed to the surface of the carbon steel during the immersion process. The Gaussian fitting shows the C 1 s and the N 1 s spectra in Fig. 7c and 7d, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum can be fitted into three peaks, with 284.90 eV, 286.88 eV, and 288.68 eV, respectively. It corresponds to the covalent bond of C = O, O − C = OH, and C − N respectively, proving that the NCDs inhibitor exists on the surface of carbon steel. In addition, the N 1 s spectrum can be fitted to three peaks of 398.58 eV, 400.23 eV, and 401.78 eV, which can be understood as C − N bond without protonation with solution, N − Fe bond formed by adsorption with Fe, and N+H bond formed by hydrogen bond, respectively [52]. Therefore, it can be proved that the N element in the corrosion inhibitor NCDs is coordinated with the unoccupied d orbital of Fe due to its rich electron, which forms the inhibition film to hinder the corrosion of carbon steel. To further explore the adsorption characteristics of corrosion inhibitors, the data of EIS fits the adsorption isotherm, and then the Gibbs free energy of the reaction is obtained.

The adsorption isotherm is acquired from equation (7) [53]:
(7)
C inh
θ
=
1
K ads
+
C inh

where C inh is the concentration of corrosion inhibitor, θ represents the surface coverage, and K ads is the adsorption isotherm constant. Fig. 8 shows the linear curve of C and C/θ calculated and fitted by EIS data. The correlation coefficient R 2 is 0.9997, indicating a good correlation between the fitting curve. It further illustrates that the adsorption process of corrosion inhibitor follows Langmuir adsorption. By calculation, the K ads value of NCDs inhibitor is 1083.07 g/L. The following equation can calculate the Gibbs free energy (
Δ
G
ads
0
) [26]:
(8)
Δ
G
ads
0
= - R T ln
( 1000
K ads
)

wherein, R represents the molecular gas constant (8.314 J·mol−1·K−1), and T represents the adsorption temperature (298 K). Due to the measurement unit of K ads is g/L, 55.5 mol/L cannot be used in the equation and the concentration of water is 1000 g/L.

In general, the greater the value of K ads, the stronger the adsorption stability of the forward reaction molecules and the smaller the Gibbs free energy. If the Gibbs free energy value is higher than − 20 kJ/mol, regarded as physical adsorption by electrostatic interaction between molecules. The
Δ
G
ads
0
value is −40 kJ/mol or more negative, which can be regarded as chemisorption with chemical bonds between molecules. However, the
Δ
G
ads
0
value is between − 20 kJ/mol and − 40 kJ/mol, which is considered physical adsorption and chemical adsorption. The
Δ
G
ads
0
value of corrosion inhibitor NCDs on Q235 metal surface adsorption energy is calculated to be − 34.43 kJ/mol, so it is proved that the adsorption methods between the two include two kinds of physical and chemical adsorption. Combining the previous UV–vis, FTIR and XPS spectra confirms that the corrosion inhibitor NCDs contain electron-rich N, O, and electron-rich conjugated structures. Therefore, it can be regarded as the NCDs corrosion inhibitor contains electron-rich functional groups that coordinate with metals. The chelating forms complexes and produces the dense protective film to isolate the reaction between the metal surface and the external environment, thereby preventing the corrosion of metals.

The surface morphology of the carbon steel is captured by the SEM after polishing and immersion for 6 h in 1 M HCl solution without corrosion inhibitor and containing 100 mg/L of the NCDs inhibitor. The SEM images are illustrated in Fig. 9 a ∼ 9c. The surface of the non-immersed carbon steel after polishing is described in Fig. 9a, which has no evident corrosion and some faint scratches. Fig. 9b represents the surface of carbon steel immersed in 1 M HCl solution for 6 h. It is not difficult to find that the surface of carbon steel is corroded seriously, and there is lots of iron oxidation and chloride generation on the surface. The addition of 100 mg/L NCDs significantly weakened the surface corrosion, and the surface is absorbed the compact adsorption film. After comparison, the observation results confirm that the addition of corrosion inhibitors increases the adsorption film coverage on the metal surface. At the same time, the corresponding EDS diagram comparison shows that the Fe content on the metal surface decreases after adding corrosion inhibitor NCDs, indicating that the surface coverage increases. On the other hand, the metal surface of the immersion inhibitor shows the N element, which further clarified that the corrosion inhibitor and metal iron formed the coordination bond, forming the chelate barrier film. Due to investigate the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of the surface, the contact angle of the three different conditions is measured. The surface of blank carbon steel shows a shallow contact Angle (27°). The surface has high hydrophilicity, which indicates that more free Fe ions on the corroded surface are easy to cooperate with water molecules in solution. However, the carbon steel surface at the 100 mg/L of NCDs displays a higher contact angle (49°) than that of the blank. This result indicates that there is competitive adsorption between inhibitor molecules and water molecules in solution. The relative volume and mass of inhibitor molecules are much higher than that of water molecules. Thus, the van der Waals force and electrostatic force between inhibitor molecules and iron ions are better than water molecules. The increase of the adsorption probability of corrosion inhibitors increases the hydrophobicity of the metal surface.

Fig. 9g presents the rough surface of carbon steel surface in three different states by AFM image. As shown in Fig. 9g, the surface of the blank sample shows a humpy-like irregular surface, indicating that severe damage occurs on the metal steel surface in 1 M HCl solution. In contrast, the carbon steel immersed in the solution containing corrosion inhibitor exhibits a much smoother surface. This indicates that the carbon steel surface is adsorbed by corrosion inhibitors which form compact barrier film to reduce the corrosion of the metal. Through the AFM technique, the surface roughness values of polished steel, the 1 M HCl solution immersed steel, and 100 mg/L of NCDs immersed steel are 33.71 nm, 191.5 nm, and 58.67 nm, respectively. The surface roughness of metal with corrosion inhibitor is significantly lower than that of HCl solution without corrosion inhibitor. Comprehensive above, the surface smoothness, hydrophobicity increase, and roughness decrease of carbon steel with corrosion inhibitor. These surface phenomena indicate that corrosion inhibitors can effectively weaken the corrosion of carbon steel by hydrochloric acid. The results of the surface analysis are in agreement with those of the electrochemical test. It is suggested that the NCDs inhibition can be accumulated on the metal surface to reduce the activity of metal reaction, further achieving the effect of inhibiting corrosion.

The adsorption mechanism of the NCDs corrosion inhibitor in 1 M HCl on the surface of carbon steel is shown in Fig. 10 . According to the isothermal adsorption analysis, it has been known that the NCDs exist on the surface of carbon steel in the form of physical adsorption and chemical adsorption respectively. The C 1 s spectrum and N 1 s spectrum of XPS on the surface of the NCDs impregnated carbon steel (Fig. 7c-7d) show the presence of oxygen functional groups and N − Fe bond on the surface. It indicates that the lone pair electron of the nitrogen atom in the NCDs compound is coordinated with the unoccupied 3d orbital of the Fe atom. The unbonded orbital of the Fe atom containing electrons is counter-coordinated with the empty orbital of NCDs compound to form a stable chelate. In addition, electrostatic interactions between NCDs compounds and free metal ions allow them to further adsorb and aggregate on the surface of the metal. According to the electrochemical data, with the increase of the concentration of NCDs, the adsorption film on the surface occupies a larger area, which hinders the reaction of active ions in the corrosive medium with metals. In short, the mechanism of corrosion inhibition can be considered that the NCDs with the structure of nitrogen oxide graphene generate electrostatic interaction with the metal surface and at the same time strengthens the adsorption force through the chemical bond to achieve good corrosion inhibition performance.

The structure and morphology of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) prepared using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors have been investigated extensively. What is more, the corrosion effect and mechanism of the NCDs on Q235 steel in 1 M HCl solution were thoroughly studied. The main conclusions are as follows:
(1)
UV–vis spectrum and FTIR spectrum revealed that the NCDs contained electron-rich conjugated structures and functional groups containing amide structures.
(2)
The results of electrochemical measurement displayed that NCDs inhibitor belonged to mixed typed inhibitor dominated by anodic behavior. The inhibition efficiency was up to 93% at 100 mg/L of NCDs in 1 M HCl solution.
(3)
The fitting data of EIS indicated that the adsorption of the NCDs on the surface of carbon steel followed Langmuir adsorption isotherm belonged to physical and chemical adsorption.
(4)
The surface analysis confirmed that the surface of carbon steel could be formed by corrosion inhibitor the NCDs with dense adsorption film.

Mengyue Zhu: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zhongyi He: Supervision, Visualization. Lei Guo: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Renhui Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Valentine Chikaodili Anadebe: Software. Ime Bassey Obot: Writing – review & editing. Xingwen Zheng: Visualization, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21706195, 22062022), the Foundation of the Department of Education of Guizhou province (QJHKY[2020]067, KY[2018]030), and the Opening Project of Sichuan University of Science and Engineering, Material Corrosion and Protection Key Laboratory of Sichuan province (2020CL06).
